item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of an Apollyon with a happy ending, but I don't really think this did it for me. It's not bad in any way, but I feel like it's a little too fast-paced and leaves too many questions hanging out there.

Novote.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Typos and phrasing fix addressed! Thank you for bringing them to staff attention.

Also, as a general note, please do not use the term "grammar nazi", even when self-deprecatingly referring to yourself. It's a pretty awful comparison to make and really shouldn't be a commonly seen phrase.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A year ago I wrote here about one typo I had spotted. Now I've read the whole thing, top to bottom, and there are several of them. I have DMed Dexanote about them, but in the meantime I will list them here just in case:

- First two paragraphs of the Diary Excerpt 1: "desparate" instead of "desperate" and "cacaphony" instead of "cacophony"
- Last SCP-4005-1A rant of Interview 2: "concieve" instead of "conceive"
- Interview 3: "percieved" instead of "perceived" and "cacaphony" instead of "cacophony"
- Diary Excerpt 3: "percieved" instead of "perceived"
- Addendum 8 02/07/2028 introduction: "Acquiesed" instead of "acquiesced"

Also, there is something I haven't reported because I don't really know if it's a typo or not, but I suspect it is: in the Diary Except 2 it says "milk and money", instead of the biblical "milk and honey".

I don't want to look like some kind of grammar nazi asshole, so I'll end up by saying that I'm doing this because I want this work of art to be as perfect as possible (the irony of saying that in the context of the SCP's ambiguous utopianism doesn't escape me). Other than the typos and Dr. Hardcastle's dialogue not feeling natural to me (and even that could actually be part of the charm, seeing what's at stake in those dialogues) it's pretty awesome and deserved my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I take this article entirely at face value.

If all of this is true- if different sectors of this pan-utopian city are created based on the whims of every human- where do all the paradises created by cruel, vile minds go? Are they cut off- isolated- separated from the main city, that the scum of the earth may wallow there?

And what about human SCPs? Scratch human, what about sentient SCPs? Actually I’m specifically thinking of SCP-231. Her containment is preventing the birth of a cataclysmic entity; if her containment were to cease and she was allowed into Utopia, would she proceed to spawn said entity? I’m sure that there are relatively similar examples to be found throughout the site, but 231 is the most obvious example.

+1 nonetheless!

This is really uplifting; something you can rarely say about things on this site. I know a lot of people see this as just another cosmic trap, but I'm choosing to take it at face value. At the end of the day, I'm a sucker for unapologetic optimism and a happy ending.

One thing: it might just be me confusing myself, but shouldn't the 3rd interview be dated differently?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Does it? Are tortured geniuses so clever because they are tortured, or are they geniuses despite the torture? Nobody ever asks that, do they? None of you have ever wondered if maybe, just maybe, geniuses are made to suffer by people, and that we'd see a lot more of them if people like us didn't lock them in cages, didn't make it so that people could only express beauty through pain."
Fuck man, I don't think I've read an SCP that's emotionally affected me this much before. It's a yearning for being able to create something better made into words. I love it.
Wholehearted +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
damn i gotta say, this is genuinely one of the scariest scps i've read (though i don't think that was your intention, in the end?)

i know it's supposed to be uplifting, the ending, but god it just fills me with a reaaaal existential dread

either way, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yo thats really cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ A poster for the piece
- Here it is


Hope I captured some feeling of the piece……

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, good spot. I'll pester staff about it at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If someone is able to fix it, at the beginning of the diary it says "cacaphony" instead of "cacophony".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me reading till the end. Its alright.

item.title: +1'ed
item.content:encoded:
Read it only now but… woah.
Incredible job here, definitely. But I want to express something, so that maybe others will point me in the correct direction.

Essentially, this "city" is some kind of non-place or place where humanity, somehow, is capable of changing it according to their own will, projecting their thoughts and desires upon it?

Because, what I gather from it and why I like it a lot, is that this "city" is somewhere with different rules of reality.
A something where humans can not only with a reason and a sense, but also with a sense of their own, as opposed to the absolute randomness and "fake" histories of our world?

item.title: Re: Intentional?
item.content:encoded:
That's "Lilin" for ya, the last Angel from Evangelion.
Or better, kinda.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super interesting read and very cool how this was percieved by most (and inteded?) to have an happy ending, with many (myself exluded) feeling the exact opposite anyway. It was uncomfortable all the way through in an interesting and compelling way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really didn't think much of this article at first, but I've re-read it now after spending a week in Marrakesh with my girlfriend and it just clicks. The imagery here is amazing; well done.
Woo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of only 2 “true” Apollyon SCPs on the site, 3 counting an international one on the international wiki.

I like how it ties into the other x000 entries. While some complain about it’s length, it is about on par with the length of the SCP-4000 we actually got. Overall, +1.

item.title: Re: Intentional?
item.content:encoded:
If something is capable of eating nearly the entire population of a planet it's probably going to be considered a deity.

Actually now that I think of it that way that could be an interesting SCP:

It turns out much of humanity is all separate "parts" of some higher entity but it feels like shit for essentially killing (from it's perspective) the people it reabsorbs.

item.title: Re: Intentional?
item.content:encoded: I wasn't aware there was any sort of deity behind it :p.

item.title: Intentional?
item.content:encoded:
Did anybody else think the deity behind this seems rather Lucifer-ish?

  • People make deals with it and willfully hand over their free will, AKA their soul.
  • It's a light-bearer.
  • It takes a bunch of people to a weird place after they die.
  • Angelic motifs.
  • Many many people trust it.
  • Fire motifs (it's a lantern).
  • It generates exactly what people want.
  • People just sort of…give in to it.
  • It tried to merge Earth and it's weird after-life.
  • It un-does the events of Genesis.
  • Possibly makes people think about nothing but love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first diary excerpt and the first interview reminded me of The Dream-Quest of Unknown Kadath.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this city is like one big Minecraft map, where everyone can build whatever they want to? Expect a lot of dirt bunkers. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Thoughts.
- Hide whatever

When I first read this, something felt off. I couldn't quiet put it into words. If I were to view it through the lens of its goals, I'd say it did so pretty well. However, this itch kept crawling at the back of my mind that something about this wasn't sitting well with me. And it took a couple rereadings for me to understand why.

The concept itself — a paradise of someone else's making — is something that goes not only against my ideals of writing, but my ideals of humanity, reality, etc.

I don't like that humanity is saved not by itself, or by pulling itself out of a horrible rut, but by an anomalous object. I just don't like the idea of all conflict, all sacrifices, all struggles, being solved by an outside force. It feels so….cheap? Anticlimactic? Boring? I don't know the words — they don't seem to fit with this feeling. The idea of a paradise of someone else's making could be taken to potentially interesting places, and this does, but in a way I very much dislike — a positive portrayal. Maybe it's my own value system getting in the way, but a thing like this sounds horrifying;

I know, Martha, I studied the same people you did, but- only now do I get it. When you pass through, you… change. You lose parts of yourself, or they're transformed into something else. The denizens of this place, they're- they live as if in a constant dream, and they've shared that dream with us. One single, unified, shared dream, a wire running through our brains…

A single, unified creation, where we are all one in the same — it sounds like a nightmare to me. And maybe I just can't relate to it being a positive thing or not. Maybe I'm being too harsh. I don't know. But I can't help but feel like the portrayal veers off to what I'd personally agree with, and that it clashes too wildly with my value system for me to be comfortable.

I'm sorry if this has been rambly — it's hard to put into words how this makes me feel, but I guess that's a sign of good writing, even if I can't like or even tolerate the central premise of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gotcha would have worked if it weren't specifically hinted at in the text (the part of the interview where the two discuss trust.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goodness there's some lovely prose in here. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
D-3043 Born and raised in Buenos Aires, Argentina 11/04/05 Reported seeing a large library which stretched on forever, with "each book being better than the last." Entrance to the National Public Library of Argentina.

I found a hidden reference! Are there more references?
Anyway, I find this could be better, but it's already superb. Have an upvote.

item.title: Why I downvoted
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I'm not happy with the ending here.

+ Here's why
- Close

I find it very hard to believe that SCP-4005-1A somehow managed to convince Dr. Hardcastle to embrace the idea of the city, only she did so using pretty much the same purple prose that she did the last time. Honestly, I was starting to like Hardcastle, sticking to her guns and not wavering for any amount of proselytizing or pleading, and then she sells out just like every other person before her. It really would have been cool to have her outright deny SCP-4005 the satisfaction of getting to her in some way or another (like through suicide), or to have her simply stick to her guns and live out the rest of her life alone. The only excuse that I can think of is that she is not operating of her own free will, and that means that this SCP is not altruistic and beneficial at all.

As a nihilist at heart, this SCP doesn't speak to me in the way that I suppose was intended. It seems that this was meant to be an optimistic and uplifting way for an apollyon SCP to carry humanity into a perpetual utopia, but that's just not how I could interpret the situation. The bottom line is that all of humanity just XK'd themselves to a cognitohazardous lamp1 that convinced them to dematerialize themselves to another plane of existence that they ultimately know nothing about because they have no real experience with it.

Overall, I'm just not liking the happy ending that this is going for, especially when there is evidence that points to something sinister happening, only for it to never be capitalized upon, or honestly even acknowledged in any real way besides in speculation by Dr. Hardcastle in the interviews. It's well written, but I've got to downvote.

EDIT: a comma

Footnotes
1. All evidence leads me to conclude that it IS a cognitohazard, not a choice, such as the spontaneous generation of -1 instances despite the lamp and other -1s being isolated from specific populations and the sudden uncharacteristic gullibility of Dr. Hardcastle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to have to give this a -1. It's basically the same thing as SCP-3519 (with which I already have subjective issues that I won't reiterate here; go see the review I posted on 3519 if you'd like my thoughts on the matter) with a more religious twist; the diary entries are a clever idea, and I do appreciate that the article tried to aim for a more optimistic tone, but they don't do nearly enough to save it in my eyes. Plus, while this is hardly unique to this article (or even to this site), I'm not a fan of the Hollywood Atheism on display here with Dr. Hardcastle; it's sadly entirely too common for fiction writers of all kinds to treat atheists as being eternal pessimists incapable of recognizing beauty or hope, which honestly feels somewhat insulting.

(Also, for a more minor gripe: the references to SCP-1000, SCP-2000, and SCP-3000 feel slightly tacked-on and out-of-place now that this article is officially not SCP-4000.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mistake comment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Contrary to some of the other opinions here, I absolutely love the diary entries, and would have upvoted for them alone. I think that all too frequently, long entries and logs on this site are scanned for the relevant "detail" that that the reader can find, and then say "This is the next piece of the puzzle," before moving on. Given the heavily exposition-based nature of the writing on this site, it's sensible that people want to find the relevant detail and move on.

It's far rarer that we see someone indulge in the style and writing of an addendum simply for the sake of the writing itself. Reading these diary entries was an absolute treat: seeing a writer indulging in a wonderful flowing prose that could have been shorter if it wanted to just say the bare facts, but rejected that move at every turn simply because it could.

It's stuff like that that really sells what, on the surface, is a fairly simple compulsive lantern. It's a beautifully written skip from start to finish, but those diary entries will stick with me for some time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another skip that ends with Earth completely devoid of living, normal humans. Ever since Kalinin's Proposal, SCP-3002, and SCP-3519, I kind of just grow numb to these things, even if they're written to be the most optimistic thing possible like this one is trying to be. At least this is sort of explained by the lamp possibly being commissioned by a guy who just wanted to see the perfect utopia that was described to him by the Traveler. That props it up for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Apollyon" feels tacked on to this article. Feels like it's to artificially jack up the danger element (which it was never intended for). This has the effect of making the massive effects near the end feel like justification for the Apollyon classification, and makes them lose impact.

The diary entries are just really too long.

Other than that, I liked this more and more as I read it. Very well done.

item.title: quick edit
item.content:encoded:
in diary excerpt 2, changed "context too" to "context to"
if i've overstepped my boundaries, feel free to revert my changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, this is really good. +1, and I genuinely hope this gets the spot.

+ show block
– hide block

Perhaps this is the intent, but I can't help but feel a lingering doubt about the ending. Everyone just flung themselves, willingly, into a realm that cannot be described by anyone except people who are fanatically obsessed with getting there. We only have Omar's word that the Alchemist and the Emir built the city, and if it wasn't built by them, humanity just relinquished all its power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this while sitting on the steps of a Sikh temple in Hong Kong, but I probably didn't need to; it was immersive enough as it was. A beautifully structured and nuanced article — I think I have a new favourite SCP-4000 entry.

item.title: With my best appreciation...
item.content:encoded:
Beautiful literature is a gift to all the readers, and its creator is worth of thanks. So, thanks for writing this document.It's more than a document, it's a poem of conflicts between reality and imagination, between spirit and material, between struggle and dream.

Having spent several nights of fear and excitement, I have translated it into Chinese(Sorry that my Chinese may be too poor to expression the beauty of your writing style.), and I chose "桃花石国桃花源"(The Peach Blossom Land in Taugast Kingdom) as the title of its Chinese version.

Other users in Chinese Branch are really in favor of it, not because it's about China, but because it's about the common dream of human beings from all the space, all the time.

+here are some comments from Chinese Branch.
-Thanks for writting.

考虑中分集资送Tufto来中国旅游一下

Considering raising funds to send Tufto to China for a trip.

by MottHery

這個概念很簡單又很複雜，夢想理想化，又從理想轉化成現實。筆法獨特又美妙，不安又讓人無法移目，就像身披以金絲紅錦織就的白蟒一般。

The concept is simple but complex: the dream is idealized, and the ideal is transformed into reality. The writing style is unique and wonderful, and it is uneasy as well as attractive, just like a white python made of golden silk and red brocade.

by Palewalker

我认为这篇文档优秀的主要原因正是其立意：遥不可及的希望，虚无缥缈的理想主义，努力和收获。至少我觉得这些值一个4000编号。

I think the main reason for the excellentness of this document is its intention: unreachable hope, illusory idealism, hard work and gain. At least I think these are worthy a SCP-4000.

by Diorite

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This caught me off guard and made me well up in tears
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this were shorter I'd upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is very well written, though I did find that the diary entries dragged on a bit too long, to the point that I groaned a little when I saw there was a third.

I have two other issues with it;
1: Where did this diary even come from and why was it written in code? Even a basic "found with the lantern" would do for the "where" but I can't work out why it would be written in code.
2: Why did the lanterns effect start affecting everybody? I might be being incredibly dense here but I didn't see that mentioned anywhere. And why didn't they just try smashing the thing as part of their attempts to stop it when it was affecting hundreds of thousands of people?

Also some minor notes

+ show block
– hide block

These images possess a strong cognitohazardous effect1, causing the viewer to become an SCP-4000-1 instance.

The footnote here feels oddly placed; I'd move it to the end of the sentence.

I almost didn't give it a second thought, but then I was seized with an odd, romantic thought.

Use of thought twice here reads awkwardly; suggest changing the first to "glance".

I shall need to take some time to processes my thoughts on this one I think; there's something that's nudging me away from an immediate upvote and I want to try and work out what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if it was intentional but I saw a lot of the writing of Calvino in this post, especially Invisible Cities which is a really different and unique perspective to see here. I think you captured a dreamlike meditation on thought and place and people within the constructed well, specifically within the second diary excerpt. And it was also very nice to have my worry of this SCP just being some kind of scary monster that eats people or whatever subverted into something much more human and interesting.
I will agree with the sentiment other commenters had mentioned that there is no change in argument in the final interview that would seem to rationally sway Hardcastle. It seems like you're trying to deliver some mental image or concept that Mahmoud is almost beyond words and that is hard to convey through interview dialogue but i feel like you're close. Since you make the argument in the SCP that it's not a cognitohazard but a conscious decision to embark on pilgrimage I think that if that moment of conversation could be tweaked I think it would make a great character moment to endcap the page.
Overall really great though.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a work of astonishing beauty.

The dialogue exchanges are on the theatrical side, although the incredulity on the part of the doctor plays off it well.

That's honestly the only critique I can think of. This is genuinely one of the best things I've ever read here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm upvoting this wholeheartedly, but something about it doesn't sit well with me. Both on the surface level (those interviews got pretty melodramatic!) and on a deeper level…

I feel like I have to do some sort of analysis or critique, bug me about it later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was, in a word, beautiful.

It's a delightfully positive inversion of elements from your earlier 001 (not SK), but so much infused with the intimacy of culture/deftness of Middle and Far Eastern historicity that I deeply admire in your work.

Long and winding as it was, I was enraptured; drawn through the narrative, hanging on every word - purple though it may have been - on a pilgrimage of discovery, a path towards understanding. And come time for the curtains to close?

I could not help it but to smile.

Suffice to say, it is a easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd upvote this if it were just the diary entries lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It demands purple prose. It is overwhelming.

Excuses excuses. :V

In all seriousness, this is a wonderful piece. The imagery provided for the city is quite vivid, and how this imagery changes from person to person is interesting.

I'd agree with Golden506 that the interviews and journal entries do bloat a bit, and had the fat been cut a little more the article could have been more punchy.

Still, a solid piece. +1, Tufto. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel that the word count was a little higher than it needed to be, but the ending was worth the literary marathon leading up to it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this one right up until the ending.
I was with you all the way but the last bit after the population percentage logs felt flat and kind of out of place for me.

+ contains spoilers
- hide

In the last part SCP-4000-1A says basically the exact same thing shes already said once before, only for some unexplained this time it convinces Dr Hardcastle to follow her which I thought was very out of character considering her speech about not believing in anything.

It's a no vote from me but I would have upvoted if it ended after the O5 council were converted, for me that would have felt more like a more realistic punchy ending.

Also I'd suggest removing Addendum 3 23/01/28, it doesn't add anything in terms of world building or plot, even after finding out the true functionality of SCP-4000 this part just seems like additional unnecessary information on random D-class just for the sake of padding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An Apollyon with a happy ending? Hell, now I’ve seen everything.

Take my +1 and shaddup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mm, I did want this to be a possibility, even if the narrative does very much point to the opposite interpretation :p.

EDIT:

Tufto's 5k Spoilers
aaaaah

OK I had completely forgotten that this comment existed until Sterbai pointed this out to me. I genuinely have no idea if this was some subconscious thing I buried until the idea came to me again or if this was a freaky, freaky coincidence.

So, um. Yeah. Huh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …A little more literal and less extradimensional than what I was thinking…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-2640

*X-Files theme plays*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do like that you've managed to do a wholesome Apollyon, and that the wholesomeness isn't revealed until the very end. And Addendum 6 was a very nice way of showing how the effects were escalating. But I didn't find this very fun to read at all. It was all just a bit dull. Theres a lot of well written imagery/ world building, but I struggled not to skip over the diary entries and in the end they didn't seem to add much to the story. Also the project lead being the last person to be affected by it, and Mahmoud's 'pilgrimage' being to convert the project lead, while kind of explained seemed a bit forced.

Perhaps it being both an Apollyon and an x000 contest entry has set my expectations too high and I'm being a bit harsh. Will give this another read as the contest draws on but for now its a -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lovely :)

I dont know if it's just me but it is nice to see an SCP take place outside the USA for a change, and in such a colorful and vivid fashion as well. The presence of Cairo and Marrakesh is lovely, reminding me a bit of the Bioshock franchise for a second, and how the author makes it sound like these cities could be the center of the entire universe somehow.

I'm not entirely sold on the interviews though, and idk if it was intentional or not but I think I saw some grammar errors here and there. Maybe give it a read when you're not tired and see if you find something. Or maybe i'm the tired one that sees shit that's not there.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A pessimist's interpretation of an optimistic skip:

SCP-4000 is actually the lure of an extradimensional anglerfish. Humanity just walked into its belly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, a lot. The imagery is fun, but I can't really share the opinion that this is a positive ending— Or at least, that humans are actually able to be 'happy' all the time. It reeks to me of a lotus eater machine, and honestly I don't think you intended it but that made this a very creeping kind of horror to me, because it's easy to know you'd fall into it too.

I love how this and your 001 are inverses of one another, but both based from a sort of… Rejection of the mechanics of reality. The former chooses to find and endless dream, while the latter chooses to crush hope because then the pain is at least expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting object to slap an Apollyon onto.

The journal from the author, whoof, that sent me down a dozen Wikipedia rabbit holes, and now I know that 'algorithm' is derived from a misspelling of al-Khwarizmi's name. Fatima's story is pretty compelling, but the dialogue in the second interview starts reaching melodramatic levels by the end.

Really have to wonder what D-2072 saw there. c.c

And the link to 3000 happened at just the right point, when I was thinking this was something of an inversion of the eel.

I don't know that I have a final word on this, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pack it up boys we're done here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sweeping cobwebs from the edges of my mind
Had to get away to see what we could find
Hope the days that lie ahead
Bring us back to where they've led
Listen not to what's been said to you

Would you know we're riding
On the Marrakesh Express
Would you know we're riding
On the Marrakesh Express
All on board that train

(Thanks to LordStonefish for the music suggestion)

After my incredibly depressing 001, here's something optimistic for the 4K about human agency.

With thanks to the following:

modernmajorgeneral
GermanderBassist
Modern Erasmus1
Roget
Neptune

The image is licensed to one Mike Prince, and can be found under a Creative Commons Attribution 2.0 Generic license here. It has been modified by me in order to make it look like a much older photo.

N.B.: This was originally entitled "A Lantern for Kashgar".

You can find more of my stuff here.

Footnotes
1. Special credit here because, as he pointed out to me during crit, this draft is exceptionally similar to an idea that he and I were going to collab on, which I somehow did not realise (probably because I changed this from being about creation/destruction to utopia/cynicism halfway through) and which I'm still feeling extremely bad about. Next time I swear I will pay closer attention to what I'm doing! :p.
